concepts.name: Topological Invariant
concepts.intuition_gloss: A feature of a shape — like its number of holes — that no amount of stretching or bending can change, which is why a coffee mug 'equals' a donut.
concepts.formal_gloss: A property or quantity preserved by homeomorphism, so that any two homeomorphic spaces share it; invariants distinguish spaces that cannot be deformed into one another.

resources.title: 3Blue1Brown — Who cares about topology? (Inscribed rectangle problem)

sources: Princeton Companion to Mathematics I.3 & IV.6 (invariants as topology's organizing idea)
standard first-lecture framing in Munkres and Armstrong

concepts.name: Open & Closed Sets
concepts.intuition_gloss: An open set is a region with no skin — every point has breathing room around it — while a closed set contains its own edge.
concepts.formal_gloss: In a metric or topological space, an open set contains an open ball (or basic neighborhood) around each of its points, and a closed set is the complement of an open set, equivalently one containing all its limit points.
sources: standard real-analysis-to-topology bridge in undergraduate curricula

concepts.name: Closure, Interior & Boundary
concepts.intuition_gloss: Every region of a space splits into its solid inside, its skin, and everything it can touch — the boundary is exactly where a set meets its outside.
concepts.formal_gloss: For a subset A of a topological space, the interior is the largest open set inside A, the closure is the smallest closed set containing A, and the boundary is the closure minus the interior.

concepts.name: Homeomorphism
concepts.intuition_gloss: A perfect rubber-sheet deformation between two spaces — bend and stretch all you like, but never cut or glue — making them the same shape topologically.
concepts.formal_gloss: A continuous bijection between topological spaces whose inverse is also continuous; homeomorphic spaces are indistinguishable by any topological property.
sources: Princeton Companion I.3 (equivalence as the defining move of each field)

concepts.name: Hausdorff Space & Separation Axioms
concepts.intuition_gloss: A space where any two distinct points can be fenced off from each other in their own non-overlapping neighborhoods, so points can't blur together.
concepts.formal_gloss: A topological space in which any two distinct points have disjoint open neighborhoods; the T2 condition in the hierarchy of separation axioms (T0 through T4 and beyond).

concepts.name: Compactness
concepts.intuition_gloss: A space that is 'small' in a deep sense — you can never sneak off to infinity or to a missing edge, and any infinite scattering of points must pile up somewhere.
concepts.formal_gloss: A space is compact if every open cover has a finite subcover; in R^n this coincides with being closed and bounded (Heine-Borel), and continuous images of compact spaces are compact.

concepts.name: Connectedness & Path-Connectedness
concepts.intuition_gloss: A space is connected if it comes in one unbroken piece — you cannot split it into two separated chunks, and in the path version you can walk between any two points.
concepts.formal_gloss: A space is connected if it is not the union of two disjoint nonempty open sets, and path-connected if any two points are joined by a continuous path; components partition the space into maximal connected pieces.
sources: intermediate value theorem as motivating result in standard curricula

concepts.name: Product & Subspace Topologies
concepts.intuition_gloss: Standard recipes for building new spaces from old: slice a piece out of a space, or multiply spaces together the way a line times a line gives a plane.
concepts.formal_gloss: The subspace topology restricts open sets to a subset, and the product topology on a product of spaces is the coarsest topology making all projections continuous; Tychonoff's theorem says arbitrary products of compact spaces are compact.

concepts.name: Quotient Space (Gluing)
concepts.intuition_gloss: Make new shapes by gluing: tape two edges of a paper strip to get a cylinder, add a twist first and you get a Möbius band.
concepts.formal_gloss: Given an equivalence relation on a space X, the quotient X/~ carries the finest topology making the projection continuous; standard constructions (torus, Möbius band, projective plane) arise by identifying boundary points.

concepts.name: Surfaces (Torus, Möbius Band, Klein Bottle)
concepts.intuition_gloss: The two-dimensional zoo of topology — spheres, donuts with any number of holes, one-sided bands and bottles — each a world that looks flat up close.
concepts.formal_gloss: A surface is a 2-dimensional topological manifold; classic examples are the sphere, the n-holed torus, the Möbius band, the Klein bottle, and the real projective plane.
sources: standard intuitive entry point in popular and undergraduate treatments

concepts.name: Orientability
concepts.intuition_gloss: Whether a space has a consistent 'clockwise' everywhere — on a Möbius band an ant walking the loop comes back mirror-reversed, so it doesn't.
concepts.formal_gloss: A manifold is orientable if it admits a consistent choice of local orientation (an atlas whose transition maps preserve orientation); non-orientable surfaces contain an embedded Möbius band.
sources: classification of surfaces requires the orientable/non-orientable split

concepts.name: Euler Characteristic
concepts.intuition_gloss: Count vertices minus edges plus faces of any honest drawing on a shape and you always get the same number — 2 for a sphere, 0 for a donut — no matter how you draw it.
concepts.formal_gloss: The alternating sum of the number of cells in each dimension of a CW or simplicial decomposition, χ = Σ(-1)^k c_k, a homotopy invariant equal to the alternating sum of Betti numbers.
sources: Euler's polyhedron formula as classical entry point (Lakatos 'Proofs and Refutations')

concepts.name: Classification of Surfaces
concepts.intuition_gloss: The complete map of all possible closed surfaces: every one is a sphere, a donut with some number of holes, or a sphere with some number of Möbius-style cross-caps — nothing else exists.
concepts.formal_gloss: Every compact connected surface without boundary is homeomorphic to a sphere, a connected sum of g tori, or a connected sum of k projective planes, and these are distinguished by orientability and Euler characteristic.
sources: flagship 'complete classification' example in topology curricula

concepts.name: Jordan Curve Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: Any loop drawn in the plane without crossing itself has an inside and an outside — obvious to the eye, famously slippery to prove.
concepts.formal_gloss: Every simple closed curve in the plane separates it into exactly two connected components, one bounded and one unbounded, with the curve as their common boundary.
sources: standard example of intuition vs. rigor gap (formalized early in proof assistants)

concepts.name: Homotopy
concepts.intuition_gloss: Two maps or loops are homotopic if one can be continuously morphed into the other — a movie of intermediate shapes with no jumps or tearing.
concepts.formal_gloss: A homotopy between maps f, g : X → Y is a continuous H : X × [0,1] → Y with H(·,0) = f and H(·,1) = g; spaces are homotopy equivalent if maps between them compose to maps homotopic to identities.
sources: Hatcher Ch. 0 (the coarser equivalence that powers algebraic topology)

concepts.name: Fundamental Group
concepts.intuition_gloss: Catalog the essentially different ways to lasso a loop from a basepoint and back — loops around a hole can't be shrunk away, and composing loops forms a group that counts the space's holes.
concepts.formal_gloss: π1(X, x0) is the group of homotopy classes of loops based at x0 under concatenation; it is a homotopy invariant, trivial for simply connected spaces and Z for the circle.

concepts.name: Covering Space
concepts.intuition_gloss: A space that lies over another like an unrolled spiral over a circle — many identical sheets projecting down, unwinding the loops below.
concepts.formal_gloss: A map p : X̃ → X where every point of X has a neighborhood whose preimage is a disjoint union of homeomorphic copies; covering spaces of a nice space correspond to subgroups of its fundamental group (Galois correspondence).

concepts.name: Brouwer Fixed-Point Theorem
concepts.intuition_gloss: Stir a cup of coffee however you like: some point of the liquid ends up exactly where it started — any continuous self-map of a disk must fix a point.
concepts.formal_gloss: Every continuous map from a closed n-ball to itself has a fixed point; equivalently, there is no continuous retraction of the ball onto its boundary sphere.
sources: Hatcher Ch. 1 (application of π1) and Ch. 2 (via homology)

concepts.name: Simplicial Complex & Triangulation
concepts.intuition_gloss: Build any shape out of standardized LEGO — points, segments, triangles, tetrahedra glued face-to-face — turning floppy geometry into finite, computable data.
concepts.formal_gloss: A collection of simplices glued along faces such that every face of a simplex is in the collection and intersections are common faces; a triangulation is a homeomorphism from such a complex to a space.
sources: Hatcher Ch. 0 (CW complexes) and Ch. 2 (simplicial homology)

concepts.name: Homology
concepts.intuition_gloss: A systematic hole-detector: it counts the independent loops, cavities, and higher-dimensional voids of a space by tracking which cycles fail to be boundaries.
concepts.formal_gloss: The sequence of abelian groups H_n(X) = ker ∂_n / im ∂_{n+1} of a chain complex built from the space; their ranks are the Betti numbers, and homology is a functorial homotopy invariant.

concepts.name: Knot Theory
concepts.intuition_gloss: Take a tangled loop of string with the ends fused and ask: is it truly knotted, or can wiggling untie it — and how do you prove two tangles are different?
concepts.formal_gloss: The study of embeddings of the circle in 3-space up to ambient isotopy, with invariants such as tricolorability, the knot group, and polynomial invariants (Alexander, Jones) distinguishing knot types.
sources: Adams 'The Knot Book' as standard accessible text

concepts.name: Fiber Bundle
concepts.intuition_gloss: A space built by attaching a copy of the same fiber over every point of a base — like the hairs on a scalp — where the global twisting (as in the Möbius band over the circle) is the interesting part.
concepts.formal_gloss: A map E → B locally trivial in the sense that every point of B has a neighborhood U with E over U homeomorphic to U × F respecting projection; global twisting is measured by characteristic classes.
sources: Princeton Companion IV (differential topology / gauge theory connections)
